coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical determination of heavy metals by a graphene quantum dots/porphyrin-based supramolecular system
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Sarà, Mariachiara
Abid, Khouloud
Gucciardi, Pietro Giuseppe
Scolaro, Luigi Monsù
Neri, Giovanni
coredata.dc:description: This work reports the development of an electrochemical sensor for heavy metals determination based on a graphene quantum dots/porphyrin (GQDs@TPPS) supramolecular system. GQDs@TPPS with different GQDs:TPPS ratios (ranging from 1:3–1:9 w/w) were first prepared and characterized by a series of techniques, including UV-Vis and fluorescence emission titration. These systems were used as electrode modifiers for developing modified screen-printed carbon electrode (SPCE) for the determination of heavy metal ions. The GQDs@TPPS/SPCE modified electrodes were found to be more suitable for the electro-analytical determination of Cd and Cu ions. Optimizing the GQDs:TPPS ratio, an improvement in the sensitivity, low limit of detection (LOD), and selectivity towards these two metal ions were obtained in comparison to bare/SPCE. The use of the developed GQDs@TPPS/SPCE sensors for the monitoring of Cd and Cu ions in seawater has been also demonstrated.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemical sensors
heavy metals ions
Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs)
Porphyrin (TPPS)
Supramolecular system

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Surface characteristics and electrochemical properties of GQDs@TPPS/SPCE
Electro-analytical determination of Cd2+ and Cu2+ions
Stability and cross-interference of the proposed sensor
Determination of heavy metals in seawater

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank Next Generation EU, PNRR Samothrace Project (ECS00000022) for financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV/Vis extinction spectral changes and (b) fluorescence emission spectral changes during the titration of TPPS4 4- with GQDs in phosphate buffer at pH 4. Experimental conditions: [TPPS4 4-] = 3 μM; [GQDs] = 0 – 0.16 mg/mL; phosphate buffer 100 mM, pH = 4; T = 298 K; cell path length 1 cm; excitation wavelength 425 nm (isosbestic point in the UV/Vis titration).
IR spectra analysis of bare/SPCE (black line), TPPS/SPCE (red line) and GQDs@TPPS(1:9) /SPCE (blue line).
(a) CV tests, (b) EIS spectra of unmodified and modified GQDs@TPPS/SPCE electrodes in 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3/4- and, (c) equivalent circuit of GQDs@TPPS (1:9)/SPCE.
Scan rate study on (a) GQDs@TPPS (1:6)/SPCE and (b) GQDs@TPPS (1:9)/SPCE; (c,d) linear fit of the scan rate square root vs current peak.

(a) electroanalytical performance toward four different M2+ (at a concentration of 8 µM) ions on bare and GQDs@TPPS (1:y)/SPCE electrodes in PBS at pH= 4. pH study (b) on bare/SPCE and GQDs@TPPS (1:6)/SPCE toward 4 µM of Cd2+ and (c) on bare/SPCE and GQDs@TPPS (1:9)/SPCE toward 4 µM of Cu2+. Experimental conditions: tdep= 180 s, Vdep= -1 V, and scan rate (Srate)= 0.05 V/sTests were carried out in triplicate and the average results were reported. The standard deviation (SD) is below 6.8 %.

SWV curves of (a) bare/SPCE, (b) GQDs@TPPS (1:6)/SPCE for the determination of Cd2+, and (c) bare/SPCE, (d) GQDs@TPPS (1:9)/SPCE for the determination of Cu2+. Experimental conditions: PBS buffer at pH 4; tdep= 180 s, Vdep= -1 V, and Srate= 0.05 V/s.

Calibration curve from SWV data of: (a) bare SPCE and GQDs@TPPS(1:6)/SPCE for the determination of Cd2+; (b) bare SPCE and GQDs@TPPS(1:9)/SPCE for the determination of Cu2+. Experimental conditions: PBS buffer 100 mM at pH 4; tdep= 180 s, Vdep= -1 V, and Srate= 0.05 V/s.

(a) Stability tests of both GQDs@TPPS (1:6)/SPCE and GQDs@TPPS (1:9)/SPCE at 13 µM of M2+. (b) for the determination of Cu2+, and (c) Cd2+. Experimental conditions: tdep= 180 s, Vdep= -1 V, and Srate= 0.05 V/s.
(a) Cd2+ determination on GQDs@TPPS (1:6)/SPCE and (b) Cu2+ determination on GQDs@TPPS (1:9)/SPCE in seawater.
General representation of the porphyrin ring and its ability to bind metal ions.
(a) Schematization of the GQDs synthesis using a bottom-up approach; (b)representation of the electrostatic interaction between TPPS (negatively charged) and GQDs (positively charged).
Comparison between our work and literature.
Stability analysis of GQDs@TPPS-based sensors (1:6 and 1:9 ratios) in the presence of 13 µM of M2+ during a period of 100 days.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: aCalculated as percentage referred to the initial value (t = 0).

head.ce:title: Electrochemical determination of heavy metals by a graphene quantum dots/porphyrin-based supramolecular system

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: CNR IPCF Istituto per i Processi Chimico-Fisici, viale F. Stagno D’Alcontres 37, I-98156 Messina, Italy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work reports the development of an electrochemical sensor for heavy metals determination based on a graphene quantum dots/porphyrin (GQDs@TPPS) supramolecular system. GQDs@TPPS with different GQDs:TPPS ratios (ranging from 1:3–1:9 w/w) were first prepared and characterized by a series of techniques, including UV-Vis and fluorescence emission titration. These systems were used as electrode modifiers for developing modified screen-printed carbon electrode (SPCE) for the determination of heavy metal ions. The GQDs@TPPS/SPCE modified electrodes were found to be more suitable for the electro-analytical determination of Cd and Cu ions. Optimizing the GQDs:TPPS ratio, an improvement in the sensitivity, low limit of detection (LOD), and selectivity towards these two metal ions were obtained in comparison to bare/SPCE. The use of the developed GQDs@TPPS/SPCE sensors for the monitoring of Cd and Cu ions in seawater has been also demonstrated.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metals ions (Mn+) are present in the environment, coming from either natural events (e. g. like rock erosion) or anthropogenic activities, including industrial processes and agricultural activities. Their presence highly affects the ecosystems and human health. Among the most common metals involved there are cadmium (Cd2+), lead (Pb2+), mercury (Hg2+), iron (Fe2+), chromium (Cr3+), and copper (Cu2+), and when dispersed in the ecosystem they incorporate into the food chain [1–3]. These ionic species can bind to various donor atoms, such as S in thiol or N in amine groups that are present in biologically relevant molecules, thus causing significant alterations in their structure and function [4,5]. Consequently, serious risks can arise for the population as these metal cations tend to accumulate in complex organs, leading to detrimental effects on hepatic, cardiac, neurological, and other physiological systems [4].

Several analytical techniques are used to determine heavy metals ions at low trace levels to prevent the harmful effects on the environment and human health. Among these tools, we can cite high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), surface plasmon spectroscopy (SPR) [6,7], spectroscopic techniques like atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) [8] and fluorescence (XRF) spectroscopy [9,10]. However, the previously mentioned techniques are expensive, time-consuming and need a specific expertise for their execution. Therefore, the research is directed nowadays towards the development of more fast, sensitive, and selective techniques [11]. Electrochemical sensors [3] are particularly suitable to overcome the limitations of other methods since they are reliable, highly sensitive, easy to use and cost-effective. Additionally, these sensors are portable, so can be implemented in the field for the in situ analysis of contaminated samples.

In this framework, a wide variety of electrochemical sensors in combination with different electrochemical techniques have been proposed for monitoring heavy metal ions [12–18]. The modification of the working electrode with suitable electrode materials can significantly improve their performances. For instance, the association with metal nanoparticles, organic or inorganic semiconductors, etc., can improve the detection performances, accelerating the electron transfer rate between the heavy metal ions and the electrode.

In this context, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a new class of carbon-based nanomaterials, have emerged in recent years for manifold applications, including electrode modification. GQDs show good stability over time and are biocompatible and environmental-friendly. Among various synthetic methods, either a top-down approach from graphite, e.g. through laser ablation or exfoliation, or a bottom-up approach from organic precursors through thermal decomposition, microwave pyrolysis, plasma treatment, and hydrothermal oxidation have been widely used [19].

The promising properties of GQDs make them strategic materials for the development of new technologies in various fields. In energy applications, GQDs can be used as electrode materials in super-capacitors, significantly improving their performance in terms of specific capacity, power and cyclability [20]. In addition, GQDs are exploited in solar cells, where they can increase the conversion efficiency of light into electricity [21]. In sensor development, their large surface area and peculiar electronic properties are responsible for the binding of metal ions and, consequently, the highly sensitive and selective detection of these cationic species in environmental monitoring and medical diagnostics. By functionalizing their surface with specific biomolecules, it is possible to build up biosensors for biomarker detection and early disease diagnosis [22–24]. GQDs have been also used to access supramolecular systems with a large series of chromophores and fluorophores in order to improve their sensing ability [25]. In this respect, porphyrins are a wide class of organic compounds constituted by four pyrrole rings interconnected by methine bridges. This class of compounds is well-known for their fascinating properties including electronic features, strong absorbance in the visible region, fluorescence emission bands, and the ability to form metal complexes through their central nitrogen core (see Scheme 1) [26]. This latter property makes them suitable to be exploited in sensing metal ions, through a variety of methods, spanning from optical [27,28] to electrochemical techniques [29,30]. Furthermore, porphyrins are able to form complex supramolecular structures through non-covalent interactions such as hydrogen bonds, van der Waals forces, and π-π stacking.

Several hybrid supramolecular systems based on porphyrin have been developed and studied like carbon nanomaterials [31–33], MOF [34,35] and polymers [36,37]. Among them, GQDs are good candidates to assist the formation of nanosystems with porphyrins for electrochemical sensing, especially in environmental monitoring [38–43].

In this investigation, we aim to study the electro-analytical behavior toward heavy metal ions of a supramolecular adduct between modified GQDs and an anionic water-soluble porphyrin, tetrakis-(4-sulfonatophenyl)porphyrin (TPPS). The resulting assemblies GQDs@TPPS have been prepared at different GQDs:TPPS ratios (ranging from 1:3–1:9 w/w). These nano-systems have been already prepared and fully characterized by several spectroscopic techniques [44]. Commercial screen-printed carbon electrode (SPCE) platforms have been easily modified by drop-casting solutions containing the adduct GQDs@TPPS. The hybrid GQDs@TPPS/SPCE sensors offer an improvement of electrochemical sensing toward Cd2+ and Cu2+ with high sensitivity and selectivity along with good stability and reproducibility. To our knowledge, this represents the first attempt to detect Cd2+ and Cu2+ using disposable screen printed sensors modified with the here reported supramolecular system.

The TPPS4 solution was prepared by dissolving 5,10,15,20-tetrakis(4-sulfonatophenyl)porphyrin (Na4(H2TPPS4)·12H2O), Sigma–Aldrich) in ultrapure water. For the synthesis of GQDs, l-glutamic acid and triethylenetetramine with the highest degree of purity (Sigma Aldrich) were used. The stock solutions of cadmium (Cd(NO3)2 ·4H2O), copper (Cu(NO3)2), nickel (Ni(NO3)2·6H2O), and lead (Pb(NO3)2), sodium dihydrogen phosphate and sodium hydrogen phosphate were prepared by dissolving in distilled water. All above chemicals were purchased from Sigma Aldrich.

Scheme 2a reports a sketch of the synthetic procedure of the graphene quantum dots (GQDs) using a bottom-up approach. Herein, l-glutamic acid is used as a precursor, and triethylenetetramine as a capping reagent, as reported in our previous work [44].

The nano-hybrid GQDs@TPPS supramolecular system has been prepared by exploiting the electrostatic interaction between the anionic meso-tetrakis (4-sulfonatophenyl) porphyrin (TPPS) and the positive GQDs (see Scheme 2b).

UV/Vis extinction spectra were acquired using an Agilent 8453 diode-array spectrophotometer. An external water bath maintained a constant temperature of 298 K during measurements. For the ATR-FTIR analyses, a Spectrum Two (Perkin-Elmer) single reflection ATR (Attenuated Total Reflection) diamond FT-IR spectrometer was used. Fluorescence emission spectra were recorded on a Jasco FP-750 spectrofluorimeter equipped with a Hamamatsu R928 photomultiplier tube (PMT).

UV/Vis extinction and fluorescence emission titrations were carried out by the gradual addition of small aliquots of a stock solution containing GQDs (4 mg/mL) and porphyrin (3 µM) to a pre-diluted solution of TPPS4 (3 μM) in a 1 cm path-length cell. The solutions were kept at the working pH = 4 using 100 mM phosphate buffer.

Electrochemical tests were performed by using commercial screen-printed carbon electrodes (SPCE) (Dropsens, Spain). The bare SPCE were then modified by placing 10 µL an aqueous solution of GQDs@TPPS with different ratios onto the working electrode (4 mm diameter) surface and allowing to dry at room temperature for further use.

Cyclic Voltammetry (CV) and electrical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) analyses were carried out in ferrocyanide solution ([Fe(CN)6]3/4-) in a frequency range of 105–10−2 Hz. CV experiments were performed with a DropSens µStat 400 Potentiostat empowered by Dropview 8400 software for data acquisition. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) tests were performed through an Autolab PGSTAT204 system empowered by Nova 2.1 software for data acquisition.

The electro-analytical determination of heavy metal ions was performed by square wave voltammetry (SWV) in 0.1 M pH 4 phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) electrolyte under the optimized parameters; frequency (Freq) = 10 Hz, Estep = 0.016 V (step potential), and Eamp (square wave amplitude) = 0.025 V and the pre-concentration of the heavy ions on the surface of electrodes are Edep (deposition potential) = -1 V and tdep (time deposition) = 180 s. The electroanalytical experiments were carried out in triplicate and the average results were reported.

In a previous paper, the general characteristics of pristine GQDs sample and its interaction with negatively charged TPPS were investigated [44]. Our experimental findings indicate that GQDs nanoparticles synthesized using microwave-assisted pyrolysis of glutamic acid and triethylenetetramine tetrahydrochloride (trien) as precursors, have primary size in the approximate range 0.5–6.5 nm. Actually, dynamic light scattering measurements show a size distribution spanning from small (0.8 ± 0.2 nm), medium-small (5.8 ± 0.9 nm) and large (260 ± 40 nm) particle sizes. These latter ones are probably originating from aggregation phenomena. These nanoparticles present a positively charged surface, due to residual ionizable cationic amino-groups, and some residual trien as a capping reagent. This imply that electrostatic interactions between positively charged trien amino groups of the surface of GQDs keep the negatively charged TPPS4 4− close to the carbon nanoparticles. Further, by UV-Vis spectroscopy has been highlighted that the interaction between GQDs and TPPS is pH-dependent. Studies have been performed at pH 3 and pH 7. Here, we provide further UV-Vis spectroscopy studies carried out at pH 4. This was justified by the fact that electro-analytical measurement for the detection of heavy metal ions is generally performed under this mild acidic conditions.

UV-Vis spectrum of TPPS4 ( Fig. 1a), exhibits two main bands: the B-band at 434 nm, attributed to the di-acid species, and a shoulder at 414 nm, ascribed to the free base still present in solution. The addition of GQDs induces hypochromicity followed by an ipsochromic shift, which confirms the interaction between TPPS4 and GQDs and suggests a change in the electronic structure of the porphyrin. In concomitance with the Soret band shift, an increase in the intensity of the 330 nm band is observed, typical of GQDs, and attributed to the π-π* transitions of the sp2 aromatic domains. The emission spectrum of the last GQDs addition shows a blue shift of the B band to 421 nm. This species is analogous to that obtained from TPPS4 porphyrin interacting with GQDs at neutral pH [44].

Spectrophotometric titrations have been employed to investigate the interaction between 5,10,15,20 meso-tetrakis (4-sulphonatophenyl) porphyrin (TPPS4) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) at pH 4. Fig. 1b shows a typical titration performed by exciting at 425 nm at the isosbestic point. Upon increasing amounts of GQDs, fluorescence emission of the TPPS4 species at 645 and 707 nm is gradually and almost completely quenched upon addition of increasing amounts of GQDs. This is paralleled by the increased emission at 480 nm due to the GQDs. This experimental finding is in line with similar behavior exhibited by the TPPS4 porphyrin on interacting with GQDs at neutral and acidic pH [44] and could be explained by an interplay of static and dynamic quenching mechanisms via the deviation from the linear trend of Stern-Volmer relationship. This spectroscopic evidence supports the formation of supramolecular adducts between the protonated TPPS4 and GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface characteristics and electrochemical properties of GQDs@TPPS/SPCE
ce:section.ce:para: Before to test the GQDs@TPPS/SPCE for the detection of heavy metals, their surface and electrochemical characteristics were evaluated. Therefore, ATR-FTIR analysis was conduct for investigating the surface groups present on the working electrode of SPCE after the modification with the GQDs@TPPS supramolecular system. For comparison, both bare SPCE and SPCE modified with TPPS porphyrin were also analyzed.

The ATR-FTIR spectrum of the adsorbed TPPS layer exhibited characteristic vibrational patterns consistent with literature reports [45]. In particular, it is possible to observe two bands at 1600 and 1420 cm−1, that are typically related to CC bond stretching and bending vibrations. These bands can be also attributed to in-plane deformations of phenyls in the meso position, as also C–H bond stretching and bending vibrations and out-of-plane deformations plane of the phenyl rings. The presence of sulfonic groups is highlighted by the peaks at 1151 and 980 cm−1, respectively associated with the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of O-S-O bonds.

The spectrum of the GQDs@TPPS (1:9) deposited on SPCE retained the characteristic TPPS spectral features identified previously. Additionally, a large and intense band centered at approximately 3400 cm−1 is also present, commonly attributed to the N-H stretching vibrations of the amino groups present in the capping of the GQDs [44]. It is important to note that in this spectral region there could also be contributions from other species, such as the O-H vibrations of any traces of adsorbed water, which could partially overlap the band of amino groups. A quite broad signal spanning from 1560–1730 cm−1 can be assigned to the combination of the CO and the amide stretching modes, as previously reported [44]. ( Fig. 2)

Then, we evaluated the electrochemical features of the GQDs@TPPS (1:y)/SPCE electrochemical system, carrying out cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) tests. Fig. 3a-c shows the CV test collected at 0.05 V/s in the potential range [-0.2 V; 0.6 V] on bare and GQDs/SPCE-modified electrodes, in the presence of 5 mM ferrocyanide in PBS solution. It is worth noting that the Faradic current increases using the GQDs@TPPS (1:9)/SPCE electrode. Further crucial information is determined through the value of peak-to-peak separation ΔEp (ΔEp= Epa -Epc). Since GQDs@TPPS (1:9)/SPCE shows the lowest ΔEp value (0.08 V) implying the highest electron transfer rate.

Fig. 3b-c shows the EIS spectra of both unmodified and GQDs@TPPS/SPCE SPCE electrodes under an applied potential Eapp= 0.15 V. The obtained Nyquist plot is fitted using Nova software to determine the equivalent circuits as well as the Randles parameters. As known, the semi-circle radius RCT indicates the charge transfer resistance, and the lowest value is obtained with GQDs@TPPS (1:9)/SPCE (Rp = 2246 Ω) indicating that this modified electrode has the best charge transfer on the electrode-electrolyte interface.

On both GQDs@TPPS (1:6)/SPCE and GQDs@TPPS (1:9)/SPCE, a scan rate study was also performed in 5 mM ferrocyanide solution in the potential range [-0.2 V; 0.6 V] as shown in Fig. 4a-b. Ranging from 0.025 V/s to 0.09 V/s, the Faradic current on both modified electrodes increases. Fig. 4c-d shows a linear relationship between scan rate square root and current pointing to a diffusion-controlled mechanism for the electrode reaction. In this framework, the active surface area (A) is determined through the following Randles-Sevcik equation:
Ipa(A) = 2.69× 105 A D1/2 n 3/2 ν1/2 C
where n denotes the number of electrons involved in the reaction, C is the concentration (mol/cm [3]), ν is the scan rate, and D is the diffusion coefficient equal to 7.6 × 106 cm [2]/s.

With the objective to develop sensors for monitoring heavy metals using the modified and unmodified electrodes here investigated, we tested their performances towards four different double valence heavy metal ions (M2+): cadmium (Cd2+), copper (Cu2+), lead (Pb2+), and nickel (Ni2+), by SWV technique under the following optimized parameters: Freq = 10 Hz, Estep = 0.016 V, and Eamp = 0.025 V. Fig. 5a presents data obtained from this study. All electrodes exhibit variable responses toward heavy metal ions, depending on the modification and the GQDs:TPPS ratio. In general, unmodified and modified electrodes appear to be sensitive to Cd2+, while they do not display remarkable response for Ni2+. In the specific, it is noted that a increasing the amount of TPPS within the composite leads to a notable increase in sensitivity for Cd2+ and Cu2+. GQDs@TPPS (1:6)/SPCE exhibits a higher sensitivity (1.4 times greater compared to bare SPCE) towards Cd2+ ions, while GQDs@TPPS(1:9)/SPCE depicts the best sensitivity for the determination of Cu2+ (4.5 times higher than bare SPCE). Based on the results of this study, we have selected the modified electrode GQDs@TPPS (1:6)/SPCE and GQDs@TPPS (1:9)/SPCE to determine Cd2+ and Cu2+, respectively.

As mentioned in the literature [46,47], most of the investigations focused on the determination of heavy metals occur in an acidic medium. Anyway, an optimization study of pH is conducted on the selected GQDs@TPPS/SPCE electrodes to pinpoint the optimal medium that gives the most effective electro-analytical performance for determining the selected M2+ ions. CV tests are collected in 4 µM of Cd2+ and Cu2+ PBS solutions, varying the pH level (4,7, and 9) on both GQDs@TPPS (1:6)/SPCE (see Fig. 5b) and GQDs@TPPS (1:9)/SPCE (see Fig. 5c), respectively. In agreement with the literature, both modified electrodes exhibit the most robust responses in the acidic medium.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electro-analytical determination of Cd2+ and Cu2+ions
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the findings above, square wave voltammetry (SWV) tests are performed on the modified electrodes in PBS pH= 4 containing different concentrations of Cd2+ and Cu2+ ranging from 3 µM to 13 µM. The results obtained from this study are depicted in Fig. 6a-d. Compared with the bare SPCE, both selected modified electrodes show a better response toward Cd2+ and Cu2+ ions determination in addition to a shift in the potential toward positive ones indicating that them requires less potential for the electro-oxidation of cadmium and copper ions.

Fig. 7a-b show the calibration curves of both modified and unmodified electrodes. Two linear ranges, at low and high concentrations, were noted. Compared with bare SPCE, the sensitivity of the GQDs@TPPS/SPCE modified electrodes is increased 4 times in the linear range [0 µM, 8 µM] as well as 5 times in the linear range [6 µM, 13 µM] for the determination of Cd2+ and Cu2+, respectively.

Furthermore, we calculated the limit of detection (LoD) through the following equation: LoD = 3.3 SD/α, where SD and α are the standard deviations as well as the slope. Herein, the LoD found with GQDs@TPPS (1:9)/SPCE is 0.172 µM which is improved by 1.5-fold compared to bare SPCE while this parameter is boosted by 4 times when comparing GQDs@TPPS (1:6)/SPCE (LoD= 0.436 µM) with bare/SPCE.( Table 1)

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability and cross-interference of the proposed sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The signal reproducibility, stability and cross-interference are important parameters for the practical applicability of the sensors. The stability parameters were tested and the results obtained are grouped in Fig. 8a. As can be seen, both modified GQDs@/SPCE sensors show moderate stability at 100 days after the initial test. For a more precise quantification of this parameter, please refer to Table 2, where numerical stability values calculated for the same electrode at regular time intervals are given.

Then, the interference of Cd2+ in the determination of Cu2+ ions using the GQDs@TPPS (1:9)/SPCE sensor was investigated. The results collected are presented in Fig. 8b-c. For Cu2+ at the concentration of 8 µM, an oxidation peak is observed at −0.13 V. When adding Cd2+ at the same concentration of Cu2+, the potential peak of Cu2+ shift to −0.21 V, but no remarkable interference was observed. Vice versa, in the case of Cd2+ determination with GQDs@TPPS (1:6)/SPCE sensor, the influence of Cu2+ is dramatic, leading to a strong suppression of the Cd2+ peak.

The above observations reveals that the GQDs@TPPS/SPCE system highlighted a marked preference for the Cu2+ ion, resulting in agreement with the existing literature data [52], which indicate this well-defined order for the easy insertion of metal ions into porphyrin at pH 4: Cu(II) > Zn(II) > Co(II) > Mn(II) > Ni(II). Based on the experimental and literature findings, we can assume that the Cu porphyrin core is stable enough to prevent the replacement of Cu2+ with Cd2+.

To explain this behaviour, we can invoke the factors that contribute to the chemical affinity between GQDs@TPPS and Cu2+, such as the formation of coordination bonds. The nitrogen sites present in the TPPS structure can act as ligands for the Cu2+ ion, forming stable complexes. The coordination geometry of Cu2+ further favours the interaction with the nitrogen ligands of the TPPS and the ionic dimensions: The Cu2+ ion has a smaller ionic radius than Cd2+, allowing it to adapt better to the internal cavity of the porphyrin and stabilize better the complex with the former ion, and imply that the combination of the porphyrin binding site with GQDs may be an ideal strategy for the design of highly sensitive and selective Cu2+ chemo-sensors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of heavy metals in seawater
ce:section.ce:para: Owing the good performances of the GQDs@TPPS/SPCE modified electrodes, it was checked their efficiency in the determination of heavy metal ions a real sample of seawater collected in Messina, Italy and analyzed without any pre-treatment. Indeed, the major goal of this investigation is the determination of Pb2+ in seawater (pH = 8.05) without any pre-treatment, as reported in a recent our paper [47]. Fig. 9a-b reports data for the analysis of Cd2+ and Cu2+ ions in seawater. As expected, in these conditions, the responses are much lower than obtained in the optimal conditions at pH 4.5. Anyway, the response to both metals in seawater is almost similar to that obtained in PBS at pH between 6 and 9, suggesting that the matrix effect is not a limiting factor with these sensors.

These findings confirm that the modified electrodes are very promising for the direct determination of the M2+ ions like cadmium and lead, in seawater, so we have planned additional tests for optimizing the analysis procedure for the practical actuation in these desired conditions.

An electrochemical sensor has been developed by using GQDs@TPPS hybrid. The modified electrode showed an excellent electro-analytical property in the determination of heavy metals. Indeed, the modified SPCE based on the supramolecular nano-system GQDs@TPPS with two ratios (1:6) and (1:9) works well for the determination of Cu2+ and Cd2+. Compared with bare/SPCE, the sensitivity of GQDs@TPPS (1:6)/SPCE is increased about ∼ 5 times toward Cd2+ ions while using GQDs@TPPS (1:9)/SPCE this feature is improved about 4 times toward Cu2+. Cross-sensitivity tests demonstrated that Cu2+ is not influenced by Cd2+ ions, implying that the TPPS porphyrin forms more stable complexes with the former ion. Analysis of these ions in seawater with no pretreatment suggests that the developed sensors are promising for environmental applications.

Khouloud Abid: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis. Pietro Giuseppe Gucciardi: Visualization. Luigi Monsù Scolaro: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Giovanni Neri: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Conceptualization. Mariachiara Sarà: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank Next Generation EU, PNRR Samothrace Project (ECS00000022) for financial support.
